meta.toolResultPreview: 1: # Gale 2: 3: Fast, isolated package management for developers. 4: Versioned installs, per-project environments that 5: activate automatically. 6: 7: ## Why 8: 9: Homebrew is easy to start with and hard to maintain. 10: Dependencies pile up invisibly, there is no manifest 11: to version-control, and a clean reinstall means 12: starting over. Nix solves all of this — declarative 13: config, rollback, per-project isolation — but demands 14: you learn a language and debug cryptic build failures. 15: Gale gives you the declarative model without the 16: complexity. Gale is as easy to use as Homebrew and as predictable 17: as Nix, without the baggage of either. 18: 19: ## Install 20: 21: ```sh 2…

meta.toolResultPreview: 1: # Design 2: 3: ## What Gale Is 4: 5: Gale is a package manager for developer CLI tools. 6: It replaces three tools with one: 7: 8: - **Homebrew** — global package installation 9: - **Nix / home-manager** — declarative package manifests 10: - **direnv + Nix flakes** — per-project environments 11: 12: Gale is inspired by Nix and Homebrew but is not a clone 13: of either. It takes the best ideas from both — Nix's 14: declarative model, Homebrew's simplicity — and refines 15: them into something smaller and more opinionated. 16: 17: ## Principles 18: 19: **Everything from source.** Every recipe defines how 20: to build from source. Prebuilt binaries (GHCR cache) 21: are an optimization, not a…

meta.toolResultPreview: 220: ## Bootstrap 221: 222: Fresh machine setup: 223: 224: ```bash 225: # Install gale binary 226: # Add to .zshrc: 227: export PATH="$HOME/.gale/current/bin:$PATH" 228: eval "$(gale hook direnv)" 229: # Copy manifest from dotfiles: 230: cp ~/dotfiles/gale.toml ~/.gale/gale.toml 231: # Install everything: 232: gale sync 233: ``` 234: 235: After sync, direnv is available (it's a gale package), 236: and project environments activate on `cd`. 237:

meta.toolResultPreview: 1: # Style Guide 2: 3: ## Writing 4: 5: Follow Strunk & White. Omit needless words. Use active 6: voice. Make definite assertions. 7: 8: Short sentences. One idea per sentence. If a sentence 9: has a semicolon, consider splitting it. 10: 11: ## Documentation 12: 13: ### Man pages 14: 15: Write in OpenBSD mandoc style: 16: 17: - Terse. One fact per sentence. 18: - Imperative mood for commands: "Install a package" 19: not "This command installs a package." 20: - No marketing language. Describe behavior. 21: - Consistent grammar across all entries. 22: - Use mdoc macros: `.Nm`, `.Ar`, `.Fl`, `.Ev`, `.Pa`. 23: - Include an EXAMPLES section with practical, 24: copy-paste-ready commands. Enough to…

vision.rationale: The README.md provides the public vision (replace Homebrew and Nix with one simple declarative tool), and docs/dev/design.md provides deep architectural vision including principles, terminology, directory layout rationale, and philosophy around static linking and source builds.

architecture.rationale: docs/dev/design.md covers the full architecture: store/generation/current model, atomic swap mechanism, global vs project environments, install flow, build environment isolation, two-repo architecture, and bootstrap process. CLAUDE.md provides the project layout mapping internal packages to responsibilities.

principles_guidelines.rationale: Three complementary sources: CLAUDE.md defines coding conventions, code reuse requirements, and development workflow. docs/dev/style-guide.md provides comprehensive style rules for writing, documentation, code, testing, error handling, and naming. docs/dev/design.md states core principles (everything from source, declarative over imperative, one tool, static linking).

vision.rationale: The README.md provides the public vision (replace Homebrew and Nix with one simple declarative tool), and docs/dev/design.md provides deep architectural vision including principles, terminology, directory layout rationale, and philosophy around static linking and source builds.

architecture.rationale: docs/dev/design.md covers the full architecture: store/generation/current model, atomic swap mechanism, global vs project environments, install flow, build environment isolation, two-repo architecture, and bootstrap process. CLAUDE.md provides the project layout mapping internal packages to responsibilities.

principles_guidelines.rationale: Three complementary sources: CLAUDE.md defines coding conventions, code reuse requirements, and development workflow. docs/dev/style-guide.md provides comprehensive style rules for writing, documentation, code, testing, error handling, and naming. docs/dev/design.md states core principles (everything from source, declarative over imperative, one tool, static linking).
